ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for mixed charcoal-grilled lamb and chicken kebab platter with mandi rice. FOODON_00002473 (mixed meat dish) used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers mixed meat dishes; no specific code for mixed grill platter with mandi rice. Used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for this composite dish.

preparation_method: rice cooked with spices and broth

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter of approximately 1200 g serves 2 to 4 people; a single-person portion is approximately 300–600 g depending on appetite.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, ground, raw (FDC ID 174351); Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, raw (FDC ID 171477); Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169760); composite weighted estimate for mixed grill platter
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Per-100g values were derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for ground lamb (raw), lamb loin (raw), chicken breast (raw), and cooked long-grain white rice, in approximate proportions reflecting the dish description (40% lamb, 30% chicken, 30% mandi rice). Input record values (energy 233 kcal, protein 18.3 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 8.3 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding. Sodium 250 mg/100g is plausible given marination and seasoning. All values treated as estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; White rice (cooked, long-grain) GI ~64; mixed meat component GI negligible; composite dish GI estimated at 56 weighted by carbohydrate contribution of rice component
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. The carbohydrate content (8.3 g/100g) is predominantly from mandi rice (long-grain white rice cooked with spices and broth). Using the GI of cooked long-grain white rice (~64, Atkinson et al. 2021) as the primary carbohydrate driver, and applying a weighted dilution factor for the high-protein and high-fat meat components (which substantially blunt postprandial glucose response), the composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 56 (medium). Glycemic load per 300 g single-person serving: GL = (56 × 24.9 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.9, reported conservatively as 12.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 250 mg/100g; a 300 g single serving delivers approximately 750 mg sodium, representing 33–50% of the recommended daily limit for hypertensive individuals (1500–2300 mg/day).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt marination, limit the lamb portion in favour of chicken (lower saturated fat), control mandi rice portion size, and avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments to keep total meal sodium within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mandi rice is a refined carbohydrate with a medium composite GI (~56); however, the high protein (18.3 g/100g) and fat (15 g/100g) content significantly blunts postprandial glucose response, and sugar is very low at 0.4 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should practise strict portion control on the mandi rice component, favour the grilled chicken over lamb, and consider requesting brown rice or reducing rice quantity to lower the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.8 g/100g (predominantly from lamb) and cholesterol at 58 mg/100g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 17.4 g saturated fat, approaching the recommended daily limit of 20 g for many adults.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb portions and favour the chicken component, avoid adding butter or ghee to the rice, request charcoal grilling without added fat, and limit serving size to 200–250 g to keep saturated fat intake within cardiovascular-safe thresholds.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine production and is a recognised dietary trigger for rheumatoid arthritis flares.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly minimise the lamb component and favour the chicken kebab, monitor nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers) if symptom-sensitive, and retain anti-inflammatory components such as olive oil marinade and yogurt dip.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Grill Platter with Mandi Rice is a generous shared platter of charcoal-grilled meats served with fragrant mandi rice, designed for 2 to 4 diners. The meat selection typically includes minced lamb kofta skewers, lamb tenderloin cubes, chicken breast or thigh cubes, and minced chicken kebab, all marinated in a spiced yogurt, garlic, and olive oil mixture before being cooked over charcoal for a smoky, charred exterior and juicy interior. Mandi rice — long-grain white rice slow-cooked in seasoned broth with whole spices including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and cumin — provides the aromatic carbohydrate base. The dish is rooted in Middle Eastern and Turkish kebab traditions and is widely served in Singapore's halal restaurant scene. Nutritionally, the platter is high in protein (18.3 g/100g) and moderate in fat (15 g/100g), with saturated fat at 5.8 g/100g primarily from lamb. Carbohydrate content is relatively low at 8.3 g/100g due to the meat-to-rice ratio, and sodium is 250 mg/100g reflecting marination and seasoning. The dish is suitable for most healthy adults but requires portion management for individuals with hypertension (sodium load), type 2 diabetes (refined rice carbohydrate), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from lamb), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid in red meat). The composite glycaemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), driven by the mandi rice component and moderated by the high protein and fat content of the grilled meats.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Per-100g values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for ground lamb, lamb loin, chicken breast, and cooked long-grain white rice. Input record values are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the mandi rice component GI from Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite mixed grill dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single entry; individual ingredient fatty acid profiles were not aggregated due to uncertainty in exact ingredient proportions. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available as a single validated entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (18.3), fat_g (15.0), and carbohydrate_g (8.3) are all non-zero. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a delivery/menu aggregator and is not an approved nutrient source under Project Veritas v2; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the nutrient evidence basis. The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore as a shared platter for 2–4 persons; the 1200 g portion weight is retained from the input record as a plausible platter size. Cultural classification is Turkish/Middle Eastern-inspired adapted for the Singapore halal market; country of origin is Singapore.
